assumptions.method: Fresh item-by-item assumptions: structured components are simulated through Optiqal's vectorized lifecycle model with skeptical confounding priors and direct harms; quality-of-life components are hand-estimated annual utilities discounted over item-specific durability windows.
assumptions.caveat: These are explicit judgment calls, not clinical truth. The point is to create a transparent, reusable benchmark that can later be upgraded to a study-level Bayesian model.

items.name: Tadalafil 2.5mg
items.personalization: Kept meaningful on QOL because current use reveals real private value, but the mortality side is heavily shrunk because PDE5 survival data are mostly observational in older, higher-risk men.
items.rationale: Tadalafil probably matters more through sexual-function / wellbeing utility than through proven life-extension at your baseline risk.

items.name: APAP nightly
items.personalization: Your March 25, 2026 home study showed mild OSA (REI 7.7/hr), and the updated airway probability is 0.695, so PAP now gets credit from an actual diagnosis rather than only wearable inference.
items.rationale: With confirmed OSA, PAP is the most evidence-backed next sleep intervention by a wide margin.

items.name: Magnesium 200mg
items.personalization: Upweighted because your 90-day combined sleep is only 6.5 h/night; small BP benefit remains because magnesium RCTs are stronger than most supplements.
items.rationale: At your baseline, magnesium looks more like a sleep-support and small BP intervention than a major longevity lever.

items.name: Custom oral appliance
items.personalization: Your home study is already in the nonsevere range (REI 7.7/hr), so a custom oral appliance is now a concrete non-PAP option rather than a speculative backup.
items.rationale: Custom oral appliance should usually underperform PAP on efficacy but can still be a credible option in mild OSA, especially if you prefer non-PAP treatment.

items.name: Melatonin 300mcg
items.personalization: Upweighted because short sleep is a live issue, but kept modest because human meta-analytic effects are measured in minutes, not hours.
items.rationale: Small, probably real sleep-onset benefit; unlikely to be a big standalone QALY driver.

items.name: Garlic 1200mg
items.personalization: Downweighted because garlic's BP signal is clearest in hypertensive adults and you are not documented hypertensive.
items.rationale: Garlic is a plausible small cardiometabolic adjunct, not a major longevity mover for you.

items.name: Cocoa flavanols ~500mg
items.personalization: Downweighted because LDL and glycemia are already good and COSMOS enrolled much older adults.
items.rationale: Some plausible cardiometabolic value, but your current risk profile leaves less headroom.

items.name: Nasacort nightly
items.personalization: Upweighted because your recent airway-directed trial improved breathing, latency, snoring, and sleep quality, producing an airway-response signal of 0.271.
items.rationale: Nasacort looks like a phenotype-specific sleep intervention here: worthwhile if nasal inflammation or congestion is meaningfully contributing, not a generic prevention supplement.

items.name: HIIT 2x/week
items.personalization: Two weekly interval sessions can add a bit more CRF upside, but the marginal return is still capped because you already train daily and your Whoop fitness proxies are strong.
items.rationale: Two HIIT sessions per week is still plausible as a small positive, but only if it replaces easier cardio rather than stacking on top of everything.

items.name: Head elevation nightly
items.personalization: Upweighted because your recent improvement pattern is compatible with an upper-airway contributor, but kept modest because your home data do not cleanly isolate elevation from the other airway changes.
items.rationale: Head elevation is a low-risk positional airway aid with the best case in upper-airway-predominant sleep-disordered breathing.

items.name: Trazodone 50mg
items.personalization: Upweighted because sleep is still clearly below target, but netted against hangover / dependency / long-run medication burden rather than assuming all sleep-med utility is durable.
items.rationale: Trazodone looks like a symptomatic sleep/QOL tool here, not a credible mortality intervention.

items.name: Glycine 2g bedtime
items.personalization: Upweighted because sleep remains an active problem, but the evidence is still mainly symptom-level and measured in modest changes.
items.rationale: Glycine looks like a plausible sleep/QOL helper rather than a major mortality intervention.

items.name: Daridorexant 25mg
items.personalization: Modeled as the cleanest trazodone replacement candidate because it targets maintenance insomnia and has direct mild-to-moderate OSA respiratory-safety evidence, but its cost is brutal.
items.rationale: Daridorexant looks like a more evidence-aligned insomnia alternative than trazodone in mild OSA, especially if you want less generic sedation.

items.name: Lemborexant 5mg
items.personalization: Modeled as a strong maintenance-insomnia candidate with actual OSA respiratory-safety data, but probably a bit more next-day drag than daridorexant and likely not covered on your plan.
items.rationale: Lemborexant looks like a credible evidence-based trazodone alternative in mild OSA, with stronger sleep-maintenance efficacy than doxepin and less respiratory discomfort than suvorexant.

items.name: Tempo run 1x/week
items.personalization: I place tempo work between zone 2 and HIIT: probably more additive than easy running, but not as distinct a VO2max stimulus as true intervals.
items.rationale: A weekly tempo run is a credible middle-ground training intervention with modest expected upside.

items.name: Nasal strips nightly
items.personalization: Upweighted because your own notes say the first night with strips plus Nasacort gave zero snoring, but kept smaller than Nasacort because the evidence is mostly subjective-sleep benefit.
items.rationale: Nasal strips can help if upper-airway narrowing is part of the problem, but they are usually an adjunct rather than a decisive treatment.

items.name: Ashwagandha 600mg
items.personalization: This is one of the few candidates I’d keep meaningfully positive because your sleep/stress profile leaves room for symptomatic benefit.
items.rationale: Ashwagandha is best modeled as a stress/sleep/QOL intervention with non-zero rare downside.

items.name: HIIT 1x/week
items.personalization: Your 180-day Whoop pattern shows avg strain 12.12 with 26.5% of days at >=14 strain, so I model one structured interval session as a modest CRF upgrade rather than a big new training load.
items.rationale: One weekly HIIT session looks like a plausible way to improve VO2max/cardiorespiratory fitness a bit without assuming sedentary-person returns.

items.name: HIIT 3x/week
items.personalization: I allow a third interval session only as a near-flat extension of 2x/week, because your training load is already high and a 2025 frequency study found no clear extra benefit from 3x over 2x in recreational runners.
items.rationale: Three HIIT sessions per week could be fine for some people, but for you I model it as roughly flat to slightly worse than 2x/week unless the extra stimulus clearly outperforms the recovery cost.

items.name: Traditional dry sauna 4x/week
items.dose_notes: Target 4 sessions/week, 15-20 min at Finnish-style dry-sauna temperatures
items.personalization: You already exercise daily and your cardiometabolic baseline is good, so I treat sauna as a modest relaxation/recovery and BP-surrogate intervention rather than as a real direct-longevity claim.
items.rationale: Traditional dry sauna is the most plausible of the classic biohacker add-ons, but the credible benefit for you still looks modest.

items.name: Cistanche 200mg
items.personalization: Small positive through possible exercise/recovery utility, not because I trust a direct longevity story.
items.rationale: Cistanche is a plausible functional-performance bet with weak hard-outcome evidence.

items.name: Creatine 5g
items.personalization: Kept positive for muscle/performance resilience, but trimmed because you are 39 rather than sarcopenic and because creatinine/eGFR make me avoid giving it a free pass.
items.rationale: Creatine has decent functional evidence, but most of the compelling data are performance / body-composition and older-adult contexts rather than mortality.

items.name: Statin (rosuvastatin 5mg)
items.personalization: Strong causal class, but you already have LDL 64 and no documented ASCVD, so most trial effects shrink hard on transport.
items.rationale: Statins are one of the more credible preventive drug classes, but the marginal benefit for a lean 39-year-old with already-good lipids is much smaller than headline meta-analytic averages.

items.name: Zone 2 cardio 2x/week
items.personalization: Because you already run and train daily, I only give a small positive for making some cardio more intentionally aerobic and structured.
items.rationale: Structured zone-2 work is still plausible as a small positive, but less likely than HIIT to create a meaningful new stimulus in your current routine.

items.name: Lions Mane 1g
items.personalization: Small only: human evidence is mainly cognition/mood signal, not durable prevention.
items.rationale: Lion’s Mane is plausible as a small cognitive/QOL bet, not a large life-extension lever.

items.name: Prebiotics combo
items.personalization: Modeled as a gut-symptom / bowel-habit item rather than a mortality lever.
items.rationale: Prebiotics may help GI comfort or satiety, but human hard-endpoint evidence is weak.

items.name: NAC 1200mg
items.personalization: Trimmed because your sleep pattern and recent response point more to an upper-airway/nasal issue than a mucus-heavy phenotype. NAC keeps some value for fatigue or secretions, but much less than airway-targeted measures.
items.rationale: NAC remains speculative for broad prevention. Its respiratory upside is much more credible in chronic bronchitis or mucus-heavy phenotypes than in nasal-obstruction sleep problems.

items.name: Apigenin 50mg
items.personalization: Kept positive only through plausible calming/sleep utility, not through a strong causal longevity claim.
items.rationale: Apigenin is a reasonable sleep-stack experiment, but not a proven life-extension tool.

items.name: Suvorexant 10mg
items.personalization: Modeled as meaningfully better than trazodone on mechanism, but with more OSA-specific respiratory caution and next-day somnolence risk than daridorexant or lemborexant.
items.rationale: Suvorexant is still a plausible maintenance-insomnia option, but the respiratory-safety story in OSA is less clean, so it ranks below the other DORAs for you.

items.name: Omega-3 CLO ~500mg
items.personalization: Strongly downweighted because this is a low dose and your LDL/HbA1c are already favorable.
items.rationale: The marginal benefit of low-dose cod liver oil looks small at your baseline risk.

items.name: Doxepin 3mg
items.personalization: Modeled as a better-targeted sleep-maintenance bridge than trazodone, but still with some hangover and respiratory caution rather than assuming it is free upside.
items.rationale: Low-dose doxepin has better insomnia-guideline support than trazodone for sleep maintenance, but it remains a symptom treatment rather than an airway fix.

items.name: Humidifier nightly
items.personalization: Kept small because your current evidence points more to upper-airway obstruction and nasal inflammation than to dry-air irritation alone; nasal-inflammation probability is 0.469.
items.rationale: A bedroom humidifier is modeled as a modest nasal-comfort adjunct, not a real OSA treatment. It is most attractive when the room is actually dry or you wake with dry irritated nasal passages.

items.name: NR 300mg
items.personalization: I kept a small positive here only because there is at least one recent long-COVID RCT signal, but in healthier adults the literature is mostly NAD+ biomarker movement without obvious clinical payoff.
items.rationale: NR is still mostly a mechanistic bet, with a small possible symptom pathway for fatigue / recovery.

items.name: NR 300mg (unbundled)
items.personalization: Same biology as bundled NR; the question here is whether it is worth buying as a standalone product.
items.rationale: Standalone NR should inherit the same tiny clinical estimate as bundled NR, with cost deciding the verdict.

items.name: Strength maintenance
items.personalization: Near flat because your described routine already contains daily strength work, so a separate strength-maintenance intervention adds little.
items.rationale: Strength is important in general, but your marginal gain from formalizing it further appears small.

items.name: Urolithin A 500mg
items.personalization: I kept a small functional upside for exercise recovery / mitochondria, but not enough to justify a major QALY number.
items.rationale: Urolithin A is plausible but still early and expensive.

items.name: Infrared sauna 4x/week
items.personalization: I treat infrared sauna as a weaker recovery/relaxation analog to traditional dry sauna rather than as an equivalent longevity intervention.
items.rationale: Infrared sauna may help relaxation or recovery a bit, but the evidence is materially weaker than for Finnish-style dry sauna.

items.name: Low-dose lithium 5mg
items.personalization: Kept small because the low-dose human outcome case is still mostly ecological and indirect.
items.rationale: Interesting neuropsychiatric hedge, but still thin as a quantified longevity intervention.

items.name: Quercetin 500mg
items.personalization: Kept small because the best case is symptom relief in inflammatory/viral-persistence settings, not generic prevention.
items.rationale: Quercetin might matter in the right symptom cluster, but not as a broad longevity capsule.

items.name: Collagen 22g
items.personalization: Strongly downweighted because the better human data are in osteoarthritis / meniscopathy, not healthy adults without documented joint disease. Any fall/fracture benefit is modeled as much smaller and grows mainly with age.
items.rationale: Collagen may help joint or skin outcomes in the right phenotype, but your baseline does not scream high-yield collagen responder. A much smaller downstream fall/fracture pathway is included with age gating.

items.name: Curcumin 250mg
items.personalization: Kept small because dose is modest and the strongest human data are for biomarker shifts or disease-specific pain populations.
items.rationale: Curcumin is better supported as an anti-inflammatory biomarker intervention than a proven longevity intervention.

items.name: High-EPA Omega-3 +2g
items.personalization: Marginally more plausible than low-dose omega-3 because triglycerides are not perfect, but still sharply trimmed at your baseline risk.
items.rationale: Some cardiometabolic plausibility remains, but the incremental value over your existing health profile is modest.

items.name: Ubiquinol 50mg
items.personalization: Near zero because the strongest CoQ10 evidence is in heart failure, which is not your phenotype.
items.rationale: CoQ10 can be useful in cardiac disease or statin myalgia, but that is not the main story here.

items.name: Ubiquinol 50mg (unbundled)
items.personalization: Same small CoQ10 estimate as the bundled version; the standalone question is mostly about whether it is worth buying separately.
items.rationale: Standalone ubiquinol should inherit the same weak phenotype-specific estimate as bundled ubiquinol.

items.name: Black seed oil 1g
items.personalization: Kept near zero because evidence remains mostly biomarker and specialty-population work.
items.rationale: Interesting anti-inflammatory profile, weak quantified personal-health case.

items.name: GHK-Cu peptide (topical)
items.personalization: This is mostly aesthetic/skin utility, which is real but should stay modest in a QALY-only model.
items.rationale: Topical GHK-Cu may help skin appearance, but not enough to justify a large healthspan estimate.

items.name: Zinc carnosine 75mg
items.personalization: Useful mainly if you have a real GI barrier/irritation problem; otherwise near zero.
items.rationale: Zinc carnosine is a phenotype-specific gut-symptom intervention, not a broad longevity tool.

items.name: Hyaluronic acid (oral)
items.personalization: Downweighted because oral HA benefits are mostly in chronic pain / joint-discomfort populations. Any fall/fracture benefit is modeled as much smaller and grows mainly with age.
items.rationale: Oral hyaluronic acid may have symptom value, but it looks phenotype-specific. A much smaller downstream fall/fracture pathway is included with age gating.

items.name: Vitamin K2 MK-7+MK-4
items.personalization: Kept near zero because the more favorable fracture/BMD data are mostly in older postmenopausal populations.
items.rationale: Vitamin K2 is a weak preventive bet at your age unless there is a clearer bone-risk story.

items.name: Lutein+Zeaxanthin
items.personalization: Downweighted because AREDS2 is secondary prevention in older adults with existing AMD risk, not primary prevention for a 39-year-old.
items.rationale: Reasonable eye-health hedge, but the extrapolation to you is thin.

items.name: Ginger 400mg
items.personalization: Small positive only because human data support biomarker improvements and some joint-pain benefit, but your baseline does not show an obvious inflammatory pain phenotype. Any fall/fracture benefit is modeled as much smaller and grows mainly with age.
items.rationale: Ginger looks mildly helpful, but not transformative. A much smaller downstream fall/fracture pathway is included with age gating.

items.name: Boron 3mg
items.personalization: Near zero because I could not justify a clinically meaningful human outcome effect here.
items.rationale: Boron may matter for micronutrient biology, but not enough to give it a real QALY number beyond noise.

items.name: Broccoli Seed Ext 200mg
items.personalization: Near zero because sulforaphane has mechanistic appeal but little direct hard-endpoint evidence in a healthy adult.
items.rationale: Promising biology; still thin as a personalized QALY lever.

items.name: Lithium 1mg orotate
items.personalization: Kept near zero because microdose lithium/orotate evidence is too thin to support a stronger estimate.
items.rationale: Interesting hypothesis space, but not enough human intervention data for a large claim.

items.name: Taurine 500mg top-up
items.personalization: Very small because this is only a top-up on top of Longevity Mix, not a full taurine intervention.
items.rationale: The marginal increment from 1.5g to 2g should not look large.

items.name: Ergothioneine 5mg
items.personalization: Near zero because most of the case is still observational and nutrient-status based.
items.rationale: Interesting biomarker story, weak supplement-level clinical story.

items.name: Sulforaphane 20mg (extra)
items.personalization: Incremental-only because you already get some sulforaphane exposure from food and the bundled broccoli seed extract.
items.rationale: The extra dose is mostly mechanistic optimism.

items.name: Alpha-lipoic acid 300mg
items.personalization: Near zero because the best-supported use case is diabetic neuropathy, not your phenotype.
items.rationale: Weak general-prevention case.

items.name: PQQ 20mg
items.personalization: Mostly a mitochondrial-biomarker bet with little direct human utility evidence.
items.rationale: Should be near zero unless future evidence improves materially.

items.name: NMN 500mg
items.personalization: Near zero because NR/NMN remain mostly NAD-biomarker interventions in humans.
items.rationale: Mechanistic appeal is stronger than demonstrated clinical value.

items.name: Lycopene 15mg
items.personalization: Near zero because the cardiovascular literature is mainly observational and food-pattern confounding is hard to strip away.
items.rationale: Lycopene may be fine, but I do not see a strong supplement-specific QALY signal.

items.name: Astaxanthin 12mg
items.personalization: Kept near zero because the human literature is mostly biomarker and specialty-population work.
items.rationale: Interesting antioxidant biomarker story, but weak evidence for durable clinical payoff in someone like you.

items.name: Spermidine 10mg
items.personalization: Near zero because the better human RCT found no memory benefit despite strong preclinical enthusiasm.
items.rationale: Spermidine is still more of a longevity hypothesis than a demonstrated human benefit.

items.name: Luteolin 100mg
items.personalization: Near zero because I could not justify a meaningful clinical effect from current human outcome data.
items.rationale: Luteolin remains mostly a mechanistic / preclinical longevity ingredient.

items.name: Luteolin 100mg (unbundled)
items.personalization: Same near-zero estimate as bundled luteolin; useful mainly to compare whether buying it separately makes any sense.
items.rationale: Standalone luteolin should inherit the same mechanistic-only estimate as bundled luteolin.

items.name: TMG/Betaine 1g
items.personalization: Tiny because this is mostly a methylation-support adjunct, not a clinically demonstrated intervention.
items.rationale: Hard to justify more than noise-level utility.

items.name: 17α-estradiol (topical)
items.personalization: Near zero because this is still mostly mouse lifespan extrapolation.
items.rationale: No strong basis for a meaningful human QALY claim here yet.

items.name: Fisetin 100mg
items.personalization: Modeled as flat because fisetin is still largely a senolytic hypothesis in humans.
items.rationale: Strong marketing and interesting biology, but not enough human evidence for a positive ground-up estimate.

items.name: Fisetin 100mg (unbundled)
items.personalization: Same skeptical estimate as bundled fisetin; standalone purchase is mostly a cost question.
items.rationale: There is still no strong human basis for a meaningful fisetin QALY claim.

items.name: Pterostilbene 50mg
items.personalization: Near zero because this is still a resveratrol-family hypothesis, not a robust human outcome intervention.
items.rationale: Little reason to assign a meaningful QALY effect.

items.name: Vitamin C 500mg (extra)
items.personalization: Incremental vitamin C on top of adequate intake should be near flat.
items.rationale: No reason to expect much marginal benefit here.

items.name: Rapamycin 5mg/wk
items.personalization: I am treating this mainly as an uncertain translational hypothesis, not a currently supported personal-health intervention.
items.rationale: Rapamycin remains interesting, but human longevity evidence is too incomplete for a large positive estimate.

items.name: HBOT 60-session course
items.personalization: I am treating HBOT as a high-cost surrogate-biomarker play with uncertain persistence, not as a demonstrated healthy-aging intervention.
items.rationale: Interesting but weakly grounded for a healthy 39-year-old; the evidence does not justify a large QALY estimate.

items.name: EGCG 400mg (green tea)
items.personalization: Near zero or slightly negative because liver-risk transport is clearer than mortality benefit transport in a supplement user like you.
items.rationale: Green-tea epidemiology should not become a large EGCG-capsule claim.

items.name: Acarbose 50mg
items.personalization: Modeled as mildly negative because GI burden transports better than lifespan-mouse optimism.
items.rationale: Acarbose is more likely to create hassle than durable value for your current phenotype.

items.name: BPC-157 cycle
items.personalization: Without a concrete injury or ulcer-healing phenotype, BPC-157 is mostly gray-market uncertainty and injection burden.
items.rationale: For a healthy user, BPC-157 should be modeled as near-zero or slightly negative until real human efficacy and product-quality evidence improve.

items.name: TB-500 cycle
items.personalization: This is even more speculative than BPC-157 in the absence of a specific recovery use case.
items.rationale: TB-500 looks like a gray-market hypothesis stack component, not a credible general-health intervention.

items.name: Berberine 500mg
items.personalization: Your glycemia is already good, so the GI downside matters more than the diabetes-trial upside.
items.rationale: Berberine is not an attractive personal intervention at your baseline.

items.name: Metformin 500mg
items.personalization: Downweighted heavily because your glycemia is already good and most compelling outcome data are in diabetic or prediabetic populations.
items.rationale: Metformin is plausible as a modest metabolic-risk intervention, but not a big generic longevity lever for your phenotype.

items.name: Finasteride 1.25mg
items.personalization: Modeled almost entirely as QOL: hair preservation seems clearly valued, but I netted that against sexual-side-effect risk rather than assuming pure upside.
items.rationale: Hair-loss treatment has meaningful psychosocial value for some men, but very little credible mortality effect.

items.name: SGLT2i (empagliflozin)
items.personalization: Transport is extremely weak here because the flagship benefits are in diabetes, heart failure, and CKD populations rather than a healthy lean 39-year-old.
items.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors are clinically important in the right phenotype, but they should not look like a major longevity drug for you.

items.name: Low-dose aspirin 81mg
items.personalization: The bleeding downside transports better to you than the net-prevention upside, because you are young and low-risk rather than high-ASCVD.
items.rationale: Low-dose aspirin is now mostly a narrow-risk tool, not a generic prevention default.

items.name: Vitamin D 2000 IU
items.personalization: Almost fully downweighted because your latest 25(OH)D is 51.5 ng/mL, already in a replete range.
items.rationale: Vitamin D looks more like a deficiency correction tool than an additional-optimization tool at your current level.

items.name: GLP-1 RA (semaglutide)
items.personalization: Your BMI and likely body-fat profile do not resemble the obesity/CVD populations where semaglutide shows the clearest benefit.
items.rationale: For you this is closer to a weakly justified, harm-prone off-label bet than a real healthspan intervention.
